explanation_en: 'Mann' means 'man' in German. It takes the article 'der' (masculine).

question.en: The man goes into the city.

explanation_en: 'Mann' is a masculine noun. In the nominative case (subject of the sentence), it takes the article 'der'.

question.en: The man is reading a book.

explanation_en: 'Mann' means 'man'. It is the subject of this sentence, so it uses the nominative form with 'der'.

question.de: Ich sehe den _____ auf der Straße.
question.en: I see the man on the street.

explanation_en: 'Mann' is the direct object here, so it takes the accusative case: 'den Mann' (masculine accusative).
'Frau' means 'woman' in German. It takes the article 'die' (feminine). Note: 'Frau' also means 'Mrs.' as a title.

question.en: The woman works in an office.

explanation_en: 'Frau' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: The woman comes from Austria.

explanation_en: 'Frau' means 'woman'. As the subject, it uses nominative 'die Frau'.

question.en: He knows the woman well.

explanation_en: 'Frau' is the direct object here. Feminine nouns keep 'die' in the accusative case: 'die Frau'.
'Kind' means 'child' in German. It takes the neuter article 'das'. The plural is 'Kinder' (children).

question.en: The child plays in the park.

explanation_en: 'Kind' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The child is very tired.

explanation_en: 'Kind' means 'child'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Kind' in the nominative.

question.en: The parents love their child.

explanation_en: 'Kind' (child) is the direct object. Neuter nouns keep 'das/ihr' in the accusative case: 'ihr Kind'.
'Tag' means 'day' in German. It takes the masculine article 'der'. The plural is 'Tage'.

question.en: Today is a beautiful day.

explanation_en: 'Tag' means 'day'. It is masculine: 'der Tag', 'ein Tag'.

question.en: The day starts early.

explanation_en: 'Tag' means 'day'. As the sentence subject, it uses nominative 'der Tag'.

question.de: Wir haben sieben _____ in einer Woche.
question.en: We have seven days in a week.

explanation_en: The plural of 'Tag' is 'Tage'. In the accusative (after 'haben'), it stays 'Tage'.
'Jahr' means 'year' in German. It is neuter: 'das Jahr'. Plural: 'Jahre'.

question.en: The year has twelve months.

explanation_en: 'Jahr' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The year 2024 was very interesting.

explanation_en: 'Jahr' means 'year'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Jahr' in the nominative.

question.en: I have been learning German for a year.

explanation_en: After 'seit' (for/since), the dative case is used: 'seit einem Jahr'. 'Jahr' stays the same in dative singular.
'Zeit' means 'time' in German. It is feminine: 'die Zeit'. Plural: 'Zeiten'.

question.en: I have no time.

explanation_en: 'Zeit' means 'time'. It is feminine: 'die Zeit', 'keine Zeit'.

question.en: Time passes quickly.

explanation_en: 'Zeit' means 'time'. As a feminine noun in the nominative, it uses 'die Zeit'.

question.en: Do you have time for a conversation?

explanation_en: 'Zeit' is used without an article here (no time = keine Zeit, some time = Zeit). This is common with abstract nouns in German.
'Haus' means 'house' in German. It is neuter: 'das Haus'. Plural: 'Häuser' (note the umlaut).

question.en: The house is very big.

explanation_en: 'Haus' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The house has a garden.

explanation_en: 'Haus' means 'house'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Haus' as the sentence subject.

question.en: We are buying a new house.

explanation_en: 'Haus' is the accusative direct object here. Neuter nouns use 'ein Haus' in the accusative (same as nominative for neuter).
'Arbeit' means 'work' or 'job' in German. It is feminine: 'die Arbeit'. Plural: 'Arbeiten'.

question.en: Work begins at nine o'clock.

explanation_en: 'Arbeit' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: The work is fun for me.

explanation_en: 'Arbeit' means 'work'. As the sentence subject, it uses the nominative 'die Arbeit'.

question.en: He is looking for a new job.

explanation_en: 'Arbeit' here means 'job'. After 'eine' (feminine indefinite accusative), 'Arbeit' is the direct object. Feminine accusative: 'eine Arbeit'.
'Schule' means 'school' in German. It is feminine: 'die Schule'. Plural: 'Schulen'.

question.en: School starts at eight o'clock.

explanation_en: 'Schule' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: The child goes to school.

explanation_en: 'in die Schule' means 'to school'. After 'in' with direction of movement, the accusative is used: 'die Schule' (feminine accusative).

question.de: Es gibt viele _____ in dieser Stadt.
question.en: There are many schools in this city.

explanation_en: The plural of 'Schule' is 'Schulen'. After 'viele' (many), use the plural form.
'Stadt' means 'city' or 'town' in German. It is feminine: 'die Stadt'. Plural: 'Städte' (with umlaut).

question.en: Berlin is a big city.

explanation_en: 'Stadt' means 'city/town'. It is feminine: 'eine Stadt' (feminine indefinite nominative).

question.en: The city has many parks.

explanation_en: 'Stadt' means 'city'. As a feminine noun, it uses 'die Stadt' in the nominative.

question.de: Er fährt in die _____ zum Einkaufen.
question.en: He goes into the city to go shopping.

explanation_en: 'in die Stadt' uses accusative because there is movement toward the city (direction). Feminine accusative: 'die Stadt'.
'Land' means 'country' in German. It is neuter: 'das Land'. Plural: 'Länder' (with umlaut). It can also mean 'countryside'.

question.en: Germany is a large country.

explanation_en: 'Land' means 'country'. It is neuter: 'ein Land' (neuter indefinite nominative).

question.en: The country has many inhabitants.

explanation_en: 'Land' means 'country'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Land' in the nominative.

question.en: There are many countries in Europe.

explanation_en: The plural of 'Land' is 'Länder' (with umlaut). After 'viele' (many), use the plural form.
'Geld' means 'money' in German. It is neuter: 'das Geld'. It is usually used without a plural (uncountable).

question.en: I have no money.

explanation_en: 'Geld' means 'money'. It is neuter: 'das Geld', 'kein Geld' (no money — neuter accusative).

question.en: The money is on the table.

explanation_en: 'Geld' means 'money'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Geld' in the nominative.

question.en: She gives him the money back.

explanation_en: 'Geld' is the accusative direct object here ('gives back what?'). Neuter accusative: 'das Geld' (same form as nominative).
'Auto' means 'car' in German. It is neuter: 'das Auto'. Plural: 'Autos'. Short for 'Automobil'.

question.en: The car is red.

explanation_en: 'Auto' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The car is parked in front of the house.

explanation_en: 'Auto' means 'car'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Auto' in the nominative.

question.en: He is buying a new car.

explanation_en: 'Auto' is the accusative direct object. Neuter nouns take 'ein Auto' in the accusative (same as nominative for neuter).
'Tisch' means 'table' in German. It is masculine: 'der Tisch'. Plural: 'Tische'.

question.en: The book is on the table.

explanation_en: 'Tisch' means 'table'. After 'auf dem' (on the — dative), masculine nouns use 'dem Tisch'.

question.en: The table is made of wood.

explanation_en: 'Tisch' means 'table'. As a masculine noun, it uses 'der Tisch' in the nominative.

question.de: Sie stellen das Essen auf den _____ .
question.en: They put the food on the table.

explanation_en: 'auf den Tisch' uses the accusative because there is movement (putting something onto the table). Masculine accusative: 'den Tisch'.
'Tür' means 'door' in German. It is feminine: 'die Tür'. Plural: 'Türen'.

question.en: The door is open.

explanation_en: 'Tür' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: Please close the door.

explanation_en: 'Tür' means 'door'. 'die Tür' is the accusative direct object (close what? the door). Feminine accusative = same as nominative: 'die Tür'.

question.en: He knocks on the door.

explanation_en: 'an die Tür' uses the accusative because of directed movement (knocking toward/at the door). Feminine accusative: 'die Tür'.
'Straße' means 'street' in German. It is feminine: 'die Straße'. Plural: 'Straßen'. The ß (Eszett) represents a long 's' sound.

question.en: The street is very loud.

explanation_en: 'Straße' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: The child plays on the street.

explanation_en: 'auf der Straße' uses the dative (location, not movement). Feminine dative: 'der Straße'.

question.en: He lives in the next street.

explanation_en: 'in der nächsten Straße' uses the dative (location). Feminine dative: 'der Straße'.
'Familie' means 'family' in German. It is feminine: 'die Familie'. Plural: 'Familien'.

question.en: The family lives in Munich.

explanation_en: 'Familie' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'die'.

question.en: My family is very big.

explanation_en: 'Familie' means 'family'. With possessive 'meine' (my — feminine nominative), it remains 'meine Familie'.

question.en: He visits his family at the weekend.

explanation_en: 'Familie' is the accusative direct object. With 'seine' (his — feminine accusative), it stays 'seine Familie'.
'Freund' means 'friend' (male) in German. It is masculine: 'der Freund'. Plural: 'Freunde'. The female form is 'Freundin'. Note: 'Freund/Freundin' also means boyfriend/girlfriend in context.

question.en: My friend is coming today.

explanation_en: 'Freund' is a masculine noun. The possessive 'my' for masculine nominative is 'mein' (not 'meine').

question.en: He is my best friend.

explanation_en: 'Freund' means 'friend (male)'. As a masculine nominative, it uses 'mein Freund' or 'der Freund'.

question.en: I am visiting my friend in Hamburg.

explanation_en: 'Freund' is the accusative direct object. Masculine accusative: 'meinen Freund' (note the -en ending on the possessive).
'Wasser' means 'water' in German. It is neuter: 'das Wasser'. Plural: 'Wässer' (mostly used for mineral waters).

question.en: The water is cold.

explanation_en: 'Wasser' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: I drink a glass of water.

explanation_en: 'Wasser' means 'water'. After 'ein Glas' (a glass of), use the noun without article: 'ein Glas Wasser'.

question.en: Have you already ordered the water?

explanation_en: 'Wasser' is the accusative direct object. Neuter accusative: 'das Wasser' (same form as nominative).
'Buch' means 'book' in German. It is neuter: 'das Buch'. Plural: 'Bücher' (with umlaut).

question.en: The book is very interesting.

explanation_en: 'Buch' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The book is lying on the table.

explanation_en: 'Buch' means 'book'. As a neuter noun in the nominative, it uses 'das Buch'.

question.en: He is reading a book about history.

explanation_en: 'Buch' is the accusative direct object. Neuter indefinite accusative: 'ein Buch' (same as nominative for neuter).
'Frage' means 'question' in German. It is feminine: 'die Frage'. Plural: 'Fragen'.

question.en: I have a question.

explanation_en: 'Frage' means 'question'. It is feminine: 'eine Frage' (feminine indefinite accusative).

question.en: The question is not simple.

explanation_en: 'Frage' means 'question'. As a feminine noun in the nominative, it uses 'die Frage'.

question.en: He answers all the questions.

explanation_en: The plural of 'Frage' is 'Fragen'. After 'alle' (all), use the plural form.
'Wort' means 'word' in German. It is neuter: 'das Wort'. It has two plurals: 'Wörter' (individual words) and 'Worte' (words in context or quotations).

question.en: I don't know this word.

explanation_en: 'Wort' means 'word'. It is neuter: 'dieses Wort' (this word — neuter accusative).

question.en: The word is very long.

explanation_en: 'Wort' means 'word'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Wort' in the nominative.

question.de: Im Deutschen gibt es viele lange _____.
question.en: In German there are many long words.

explanation_en: Use 'Wörter' for individual separate words (e.g. in a list or dictionary). 'Worte' is used for words in a meaningful context ('seine letzten Worte' = his last words).
'Nummer' means 'number' in German. It is feminine: 'die Nummer'. Plural: 'Nummern'. Used for identifiers like phone numbers, house numbers.

question.en: The number is wrong.

explanation_en: 'Nummer' means 'number'. It is feminine: 'die Nummer' in the nominative.

question.en: What is your phone number?

explanation_en: 'Telefonnummer' is a compound noun (Telefon + Nummer). German frequently combines nouns into one long word. The gender is determined by the last noun: 'die Nummer' → 'die Telefonnummer'.

question.en: He wrote down the number.

explanation_en: 'Nummer' is the accusative direct object. Feminine accusative: 'die Nummer' (same form as nominative).
'Stuhl' means 'chair' in German. It is masculine: 'der Stuhl'. Plural: 'Stühle' (with umlaut).

question.en: The chair stands next to the table.

explanation_en: 'Stuhl' is a masculine noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'der'.

question.en: Please take a chair.

explanation_en: 'Stuhl' means 'chair'. As a masculine accusative with indefinite article: 'einen Stuhl' (note the -en ending on 'ein').

question.en: She stands on the chair.

explanation_en: 'auf den Stuhl' uses the accusative because of upward directed movement. Masculine accusative: 'den Stuhl'.
'Essen' means 'food' or 'meal' in German. It is neuter: 'das Essen'. It comes from the verb 'essen' (to eat). The plural 'Essen' is the same as the singular.

question.en: The food tastes very good.

explanation_en: 'Essen' is a neuter noun. In the nominative case, it takes the article 'das'.

question.en: The meal is ready.

explanation_en: 'Essen' means 'meal' or 'food'. As a neuter noun, it uses 'das Essen' in the nominative.

question.en: I am ordering the food for everyone.

explanation_en: 'Essen' is the accusative direct object. Neuter accusative: 'das Essen' (same form as nominative).
'Mutter' means 'mother'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The mother cooks every evening.

explanation_en: 'Mutter' is a feminine noun. In the nominative case (subject), it takes the article 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ liest dem Kind ein Buch vor.
question.en: The mother reads a book to the child.

explanation_en: 'Mutter' means 'mother'. It is the subject here, used in the nominative with 'die'.

question.de: Ich rufe meine _____ jeden Sonntag an.
question.en: I call my mother every Sunday.

explanation_en: After the possessive 'meine', 'Mutter' is used in the accusative case — but its form does not change (feminine accusative = same as nominative).
'Vater' means 'father'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The father works as a doctor.

explanation_en: 'Vater' is a masculine noun. As the subject (nominative), it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ spielt mit seinem Sohn im Garten.
question.en: The father plays with his son in the garden.

explanation_en: 'Vater' means 'father'. It is the subject of the sentence, used in the nominative with 'der'.

question.en: I visit my father at the weekend.

explanation_en: After 'meinen', 'Vater' is in the accusative case (masculine accusative adds -n to the article: 'meinen Vater').
'Bruder' means 'brother'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The brother is coming to visit tomorrow.

explanation_en: 'Bruder' is masculine. As the subject (nominative), it takes 'der'.

question.de: Mein _____ ist zwei Jahre älter als ich.
question.en: My brother is two years older than me.

explanation_en: 'Bruder' means 'brother'. After 'mein' (nominative, masculine), the noun does not change.

question.de: Ich habe meinen _____ lange nicht gesehen.
question.en: I haven't seen my brother for a long time.

explanation_en: 'sehen' takes the accusative. 'meinen Bruder' — masculine accusative uses 'meinen' + the noun unchanged.
'Schwester' means 'sister'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The sister lives in Hamburg.

explanation_en: 'Schwester' is feminine. As the subject (nominative), it takes 'die'.

question.en: My sister studies in Berlin.

explanation_en: 'Schwester' means 'sister'. After 'meine' (nominative, feminine), the noun is unchanged.

question.en: I miss my sister very much.

explanation_en: 'vermissen' takes the accusative. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'meine Schwester'.
'Eltern' means 'parents'. It is a plural-only noun and always uses 'die'.

question.en: The parents are coming to the school graduation.

explanation_en: 'Eltern' is a plural-only noun. All plural nouns in German use 'die' as their definite article.

question.en: My parents live in the countryside.

explanation_en: 'Eltern' means 'parents' and is always plural. 'Meine Eltern' uses the plural possessive form.

question.en: I visit my parents every Christmas.

explanation_en: 'Eltern' is plural-only. In the accusative, plural nouns take 'die' / 'meine' — the form is the same as nominative.
'Lehrer' means 'teacher' (male). It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The teacher explains the task.

explanation_en: 'Lehrer' is masculine. As the subject (nominative), it takes 'der'.

question.en: Our teacher always comes on time.

explanation_en: 'Lehrer' means 'teacher'. After 'unser' (nominative, masculine), the noun is unchanged.

question.en: The pupils like their teacher very much.

explanation_en: 'mögen' takes the accusative. 'ihren Lehrer' — masculine accusative uses 'ihren' before the noun.
'Arzt' means 'doctor'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The doctor is coming shortly.

explanation_en: 'Arzt' is masculine. As the subject (nominative), it takes 'der'.

question.en: The doctor examines the patient.

explanation_en: 'Arzt' means 'doctor'. It is the nominative subject here, used with 'der'.

question.en: I have to go to the doctor tomorrow.

explanation_en: After 'zum' (= zu + dem), the dative case is used. Masculine nouns do not change form in the dative: 'zum Arzt'.
'Kollege' means 'colleague'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.de: _____ Kollege hilft mir mit dem Bericht.
question.en: The colleague helps me with the report.

explanation_en: 'Kollege' is a masculine noun. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: My colleague lives nearby.

explanation_en: 'Kollege' means 'colleague'. After 'mein' (nominative, masculine) the noun is unchanged.

question.de: Ich treffe meinen _____ nach der Arbeit.
question.en: I am meeting my colleague after work.

explanation_en: 'Kollege' is a weak masculine noun (N-Deklination). In the accusative, it adds -n: 'meinen Kollegen'.
'Nachbar' means 'neighbour'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The neighbour has a large garden.

explanation_en: 'Nachbar' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: Our neighbour is very friendly.

explanation_en: 'Nachbar' means 'neighbour'. After 'unser' (nominative, masculine), the noun is unchanged.

question.de: Ich habe meinen _____ heute nicht gesehen.
question.en: I haven't seen my neighbour today.

explanation_en: 'Nachbar' is a weak masculine noun. In the accusative, it takes -n: 'meinen Nachbarn'.
'Chef' means 'boss' or 'head'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The boss is on holiday today.

explanation_en: 'Chef' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: My boss gives me a lot of work.

explanation_en: 'Chef' means 'boss'. After 'mein' (nominative, masculine), the noun is unchanged.

question.de: Ich muss meinen _____ um Erlaubnis fragen.
question.en: I have to ask my boss for permission.

explanation_en: After 'meinen' (masculine accusative), 'Chef' is unchanged — it is a regular (strong) masculine noun, not weak declension.
'Zimmer' means 'room'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The room is very large.

explanation_en: 'Zimmer' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The room has a large window.

explanation_en: 'Zimmer' means 'room'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Ich suche ein _____ in der Stadtmitte.
question.en: I am looking for a room in the city centre.

explanation_en: After 'ein' (indefinite article, neuter accusative), 'Zimmer' is unchanged — neuter nouns have the same form in nominative and accusative.
'Küche' means 'kitchen'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The kitchen is very modern.

explanation_en: 'Küche' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The kitchen has just been renovated.

explanation_en: 'Küche' means 'kitchen'. It is the feminine subject here, used with 'die'.

question.en: We have just painted the kitchen.

explanation_en: 'haben … gestrichen' uses the accusative for the object. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'die Küche'.
'Garten' means 'garden'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.de: _____ Garten ist im Frühling sehr schön.
question.en: The garden is very beautiful in spring.

explanation_en: 'Garten' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The garden needs a lot of water.

explanation_en: 'Garten' means 'garden'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: We tend our garden every weekend.

explanation_en: 'pflegen' takes the accusative. 'unseren Garten' — masculine accusative uses 'unseren' + the noun.
'Bahnhof' means 'train station'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The train station is in the city centre.

explanation_en: 'Bahnhof' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ ist sehr groß und modern.
question.en: The train station is very large and modern.

explanation_en: 'Bahnhof' means 'train station'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich fahre zum _____, um meinen Zug zu nehmen.
question.en: I go to the train station to catch my train.

explanation_en: 'zum' = zu + dem (dative). Masculine nouns in the dative with 'dem' are unchanged: 'zum Bahnhof'.
'Büro' means 'office'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The office is on the third floor.

explanation_en: 'Büro' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The office opens at nine o'clock.

explanation_en: 'Büro' means 'office'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Er verlässt sein _____ um sechs Uhr abends.
question.en: He leaves his office at six in the evening.

explanation_en: After 'sein' (possessive, neuter accusative), 'Büro' is unchanged — neuter nominative and accusative forms are identical.
'Fenster' means 'window'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The window is open.

explanation_en: 'Fenster' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The window lets in a lot of light.

explanation_en: 'Fenster' means 'window'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Bitte schließ das _____, es ist kalt.
question.en: Please close the window, it is cold.

explanation_en: 'schließen' takes the accusative. Neuter nouns use 'das' in both nominative and accusative: 'das Fenster'.
'Bett' means 'bed'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The bed is very comfortable.

explanation_en: 'Bett' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.en: The bed stands by the window.

explanation_en: 'Bett' means 'bed'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Ich gehe um elf Uhr ins _____.
question.en: I go to bed at eleven o'clock.

explanation_en: 'ins' = in + das (accusative, showing movement into a place). 'ins Bett gehen' is a fixed phrase meaning 'to go to bed'.
'Handy' means 'mobile phone' in German. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The mobile phone is ringing right now.

explanation_en: 'Handy' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.de: Das _____ ist neu und sehr teuer.
question.en: The mobile phone is new and very expensive.

explanation_en: 'Handy' means 'mobile phone' in German. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Ich habe mein _____ zu Hause vergessen.
question.en: I forgot my mobile phone at home.

explanation_en: After 'mein' (possessive, neuter accusative), 'Handy' is unchanged — neuter nominative and accusative are identical.
'Tasche' means 'bag' or 'pocket'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The bag is very heavy.

explanation_en: 'Tasche' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The bag is on the chair.

explanation_en: 'Tasche' means 'bag'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: Ich packe meine _____ für die Reise.
question.en: I am packing my bag for the trip.

explanation_en: After 'meine' (possessive, feminine accusative), 'Tasche' is unchanged — feminine nominative and accusative are identical.
'Schlüssel' means 'key'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The key is not here.

explanation_en: 'Schlüssel' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The key does not fit the lock.

explanation_en: 'Schlüssel' means 'key'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: I have lost my key.
